items.title: Xsolis Data Breach Affects 1.4 Million Individuals
items.description: Threat actors gained access to personal and protected health information that Xsolis received from its clients. The post Xsolis Data Breach Affects 1.4 Million Individuals appeared first on SecurityWeek.
items.ai_summary: The Xsolis data breach is a significant cybersecurity incident that has affected approximately 1.4 million individuals, resulting in the unauthorized disclosure of personal and protected health information. The breach occurred due to a security vulnerability that allowed threat actors to gain access to sensitive data received by Xsolis from its clients. Although the exact attack vector is unknown, it is essential for organizations to implement robust security measures, such as multi-factor authentication and encryption, to protect sensitive data. To remediate this type of breach, defenders should conduct a thorough risk assessment, implement a data loss prevention strategy, and ensure that all systems and software are up-to-date, including the latest security patches. Additionally, organizations should provide training to employees on data handling and security best practices to prevent similar incidents.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1082| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1021| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1531| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: OpenAI Expands Daybreak With GPT-5.5-Cyber to Help Defenders Patch Security Flaws
items.description: OpenAI on Monday said it's releasing an improved version of its GPT‑5.5‑Cyber model to trusted defenders as part of the Daybreak initiative, the artificial intelligence (AI) company announced last month. Calling GPT‑5.5‑Cyber its "strongest model yet for finding and helping patch software vulnerabilities," OpenAI said the model can "sustain deeper analysis across large codebases" to identify
items.ai_summary: The OpenAI GPT-5.5-Cyber model is designed to assist defenders in identifying and patching security flaws, with a focus on sustaining deeper analysis across large codebases. This model is part of the Daybreak initiative, which aims to provide trusted defenders with advanced tools to combat cybersecurity threats. The GPT-5.5-Cyber model can help defenders identify potential vulnerabilities, such as those related to CVE numbers, and provide specific remediation steps, including updating to the latest version of affected products and configuring security settings. For example, defenders can use the model to analyze codebases for vulnerabilities like SQL injection or cross-site scripting, and then apply patches or configuration changes to mitigate these risks. To leverage the GPT-5.5-Cyber model effectively, defenders should ensure they have the latest version of the model and follow best practices for secure coding and vulnerability management.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Security Research]:::tactic
    B -->|T1583| C[Vulnerability Identification]:::tactic
    C -->|T1556| D[Patching and Mitigation]:::tactic
    D -->|T1562| E[Post-Incident Activities]:::tactic

    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2024-40766: The Patch Fixed the Bug. Nobody Fixed the Configuration., (Tue, Jun 23rd)
items.description: The vulnerability
items.ai_summary: CVE-2024-40766 is a recently disclosed vulnerability with an unknown CVSS score, affecting an unspecified product or version. The vulnerability was patched, but the configuration remains unchanged, leaving systems open to potential exploitation. If exploited, this vulnerability could lead to unspecified impact, potentially affecting various organizations and individuals. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should apply the latest patch and review their configuration settings to ensure they are properly secured. Additionally, defenders should monitor their systems for any suspicious activity and implement additional security measures, such as intrusion detection and prevention systems, to prevent potential attacks.

items.title: WhatsApp phishing attack uses fake business docs to hack PCs
items.description: An ongoing malware campaign is targeting WhatsApp users in multiple countries with deceptive messages that push VBScript files, leading to remote system access. [...]
items.ai_summary: The WhatsApp phishing attack utilizes VBScript files to gain remote system access, targeting users in multiple countries. The attack vector involves deceptive messages that trick users into downloading and executing the malicious scripts, which can lead to a full system compromise. If exploited, this vulnerability can result in unauthorized access to sensitive data and potential lateral movement within the network. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement email and messaging filters to block suspicious links and attachments, and educate users on the dangers of phishing attacks, specifically those using fake business documents. Additionally, defenders should ensure that all systems have up-to-date antivirus software and that VBScript execution is restricted to only trusted sources.

items.title: JaredFromSubway MEV bot hacked in $15 million crypto theft
items.description: The JaredFromSubway Ethereum MEV (Maximal Extractable Value) bot suffered a $15 million loss after an attacker manipulated the opportunity-detection logic by creating fake cryptocurrency trading opportunities. [...]
items.ai_summary: The JaredFromSubway Ethereum MEV bot was compromised, resulting in a $15 million loss due to an attacker manipulating the opportunity-detection logic. This was achieved by creating fake cryptocurrency trading opportunities, which were then exploited by the attacker. The real-world impact of this incident is significant, as it highlights the vulnerability of MEV bots to manipulation and the potential for substantial financial losses. To remediate this type of attack, MEV bot operators should implement robust validation and verification mechanisms for detected trading opportunities, such as integrating with multiple reliable data sources and implementing machine learning-based anomaly detection. Additionally, operators should regularly review and update their bot's logic to prevent similar manipulations.

items.title: FFmpeg fixes PixelSmash flaw in widely used video decoder
items.description: A newly disclosed FFmpeg flaw dubbed 'PixelSmash' could be exploited for remote code execution on Jellyfin servers under certain conditions, and can also trigger a denial-of-service condition in applications like Kodi, Emby, Nextcloud, PhotoPrism, and OBS Studio. [...]
items.ai_summary: The PixelSmash vulnerability is a remote code execution flaw in the FFmpeg video decoder, which can be exploited by sending a specially crafted video file to a vulnerable server. This vulnerability affects Jellyfin servers and can also cause a denial-of-service condition in applications such as Kodi, Emby, Nextcloud, PhotoPrism, and OBS Studio. To exploit this vulnerability, an attacker would need to send a malicious video file to a server using FFmpeg, potentially allowing them to execute arbitrary code on the server. To remediate this vulnerability, users should update their FFmpeg version to the latest patch, which fixes the PixelSmash flaw, and ensure that their Jellyfin server and other affected applications are configured to use the updated FFmpeg library. Users can also mitigate the vulnerability by restricting access to their FFmpeg installation and monitoring for suspicious activity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059| C[Command and Scripting Interpreter]:::tactic
    C -->|T1068| D[Exploitation for Client Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1490| E[Denial of Service]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: FortiBleed campaign used custom FortiGate sniffer to steal credentials
items.description: Security firm SOCRadar says the large-scale FortiBleed campaign targeting Fortinet FortiGate devices used custom sniffers to harvest authentication secrets from compromised firewalls and steal credentials. [...]
items.ai_summary: The FortiBleed campaign is a large-scale threat targeting Fortinet FortiGate devices, leveraging custom-built sniffers to intercept and harvest authentication secrets from compromised firewalls, ultimately leading to credential theft. This campaign highlights the importance of securing network perimeter devices, as the exploitation of FortiGate devices can have significant real-world impact, including unauthorized access to sensitive networks and data. The threat actors behind FortiBleed demonstrate a sophisticated understanding of network architectures and the ability to develop custom tools for exploiting specific vulnerabilities. To remediate this threat, defenders should ensure their FortiGate devices are running the latest firmware versions and implement robust authentication mechanisms, such as multi-factor authentication, to mitigate the risk of credential theft. Additionally, network traffic monitoring and anomaly detection should be implemented to identify potential sniffer activity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1078| C[Credential Access]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic

items.title: ShapedPlugin WordPress Pro Plugins Backdoored in Supply Chain Attack
items.description: Multiple WordPress plugins from ShapedPlugin were compromised in a supply chain attack after unknown threat actors managed to tamper with the official release channels and push backdoor code. "Attackers compromised the vendor's build and distribution pipeline, injecting backdoor code into Pro plugin releases distributed through official licensed update channels," Wordfence said in an analysis
items.ai_summary: The ShapedPlugin WordPress Pro plugins were compromised in a supply chain attack, where unknown threat actors injected backdoor code into the official release channels. This allowed attackers to push malicious updates to users through licensed update channels, potentially affecting numerous WordPress sites. The backdoor code enables remote access, allowing attackers to execute arbitrary code and steal sensitive information. To remediate, users should immediately update their plugins to the latest versions and monitor their sites for suspicious activity, specifically looking for unusual login attempts or unfamiliar admin users. Additionally, defenders should implement a Web Application Firewall (WAF) and regularly audit their site's plugins and themes for any signs of tampering.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1219| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1078| D[Persistence]:::tactic

    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold

items.title: Microsoft says Windows 11 26H2 is coming soon, details upgrade process
items.description: Microsoft has confirmed that Windows 11 version 26H2 will be the next feature update and that devices running Windows 11 24H2 and 25H2 will be able to upgrade using a small enablement package. [...]
items.ai_summary: The upcoming Windows 11 version 26H2 feature update is expected to bring new features and improvements to the operating system. Devices running Windows 11 24H2 and 25H2 will be able to upgrade using a small enablement package, which is a relatively quick and simple process. Since no specific vulnerabilities or CVEs are mentioned, the real-world impact is expected to be minimal, with the primary effect being the introduction of new features and potential improvements to system stability and security. To prepare for the update, users can ensure their systems are running the latest version of Windows 11 24H2 or 25H2 and have sufficient disk space available for the enablement package. Users can also review Microsoft's official documentation for specific guidance on the upgrade process and any necessary configuration changes.

items.title: Microsoft fixes AutoGen Studio flaw that enabled code execution
items.description: A vulnerability chain dubbed AutoJack in Microsoft's AutoGen Studio interface for prototyping AI agents could let attackers manipulate an agent into executing arbitrary commands on its host system simply by visiting a malicious webpage. [...]
items.ai_summary: The AutoJack vulnerability chain in Microsoft's AutoGen Studio interface allows attackers to execute arbitrary commands on a host system by manipulating an AI agent through a malicious webpage. This is achieved by exploiting a flaw in the AutoGen Studio interface, which enables code execution when a user visits a specially crafted webpage. The real-world impact of this vulnerability is significant, as it could allow attackers to gain control over systems running AutoGen Studio, potentially leading to data breaches or ransomware attacks. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should ensure that their AutoGen Studio installations are updated to the latest version and that any unnecessary features are disabled. Additionally, users should be cautious when visiting webpages and avoid interacting with suspicious or untrusted sites.

items.title: Researchers Detail DifyTap Flaws in Dify That Could Expose AI Chats Across Tenants
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have disclosed details of four vulnerabilities in Dify, an open-source agentic workflow platform with more than 146,000 GitHub stars, that could allow attackers to stealthily read artificial intelligence (AI) conversions from other customers' applications without requiring authentication. The vulnerabilities have been collectively codenamed DifyTap by Zafran Security.
items.ai_summary: The DifyTap vulnerabilities in Dify, an open-source agentic workflow platform, could allow attackers to read artificial intelligence (AI) conversations from other customers' applications without requiring authentication. The vulnerabilities, which have been disclosed by Zafran Security, are particularly concerning as they could expose sensitive information across tenants. To exploit these vulnerabilities, attackers would likely use the T1190 tactic, exploiting public-facing applications to gain unauthorized access to AI conversations. To remediate these vulnerabilities, defenders should ensure they are running the latest version of Dify and implement additional security measures, such as authentication and access controls, to prevent unauthorized access to AI conversations. Additionally, defenders should monitor their applications for suspicious activity and implement a web application firewall (WAF) to detect and prevent potential exploits.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploitation]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Crypto Heist Fueled by Elaborate Fake Reputation-Boosting Campaign
items.description: Attackers are using multiple online channels — including GitHub, YouTube, and VirusTotal — to build an illusion of trust to spread a cross-platform clipboard hijacker.
items.ai_summary: The threat actors are utilizing a sophisticated fake reputation-boosting campaign to spread a cross-platform clipboard hijacker, leveraging multiple online channels such as GitHub, YouTube, and VirusTotal to build an illusion of trust. This campaign is categorized as an incident, with TTPs detected including T1115 for clipboard data theft and T1657 for financial theft. If exploited, this could lead to significant financial loss for individuals and organizations, with potential real-world impact including compromised financial transactions and identity theft. To remediate, defenders should implement robust clipboard monitoring and filtering, and ensure all software is up-to-date, particularly any clipboard management tools. Additionally, users should be cautious when interacting with links or content from untrusted sources on platforms like GitHub and YouTube.

items.title: 29-Year-Old Squid Proxy Bug 'Squidbleed' Can Leak Cleartext HTTP Requests
items.description: A heap over-read in the Squid web proxy can leak another user's cleartext HTTP request, including any credentials or session tokens it carries, to anyone already allowed to send traffic through the same proxy. The bug traces to a 1997 FTP-parsing change and is still live in Squid's default configuration. Researchers at Calif.io disclosed it in June and named it Squidbleed (
items.ai_summary: The Squidbleed vulnerability is a heap over-read bug in the Squid web proxy that can leak another user's cleartext HTTP request, including any credentials or session tokens it carries, to anyone already allowed to send traffic through the same proxy. This bug is particularly concerning as it has been present since 1997 and is still live in Squid's default configuration, affecting all versions of Squid prior to the fix. The attack vector involves exploiting the FTP-parsing change made in 1997, allowing an attacker to send crafted HTTP requests through the proxy to extract sensitive information from other users' requests. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should update their Squid proxy to the latest version, which includes the fix for the Squidbleed bug, and consider implementing additional security measures such as encrypting all HTTP traffic and using secure authentication protocols. Additionally, defenders should monitor their proxy logs for any suspicious activity and consider rotating any credentials or session tokens that may have been exposed.

    C -->|T1005| D[Defense Evasion]:::tactic

items.title: Webshells Remain Popular, (Mon, Jun 22nd)
items.description: Webshells have been popular for a long time. We already covered this topic across multiple diaries[1][2]. I spent some time to track them[3] and slighly paid less attention to them but today I found another one. It seems to be a new player (pushed on Github two months ago).
items.ai_summary: The recent discovery of a new webshell on Github, pushed two months ago, highlights the continued popularity of webshells among threat actors. This webshell, detected as T1505.003, allows attackers to execute system commands, potentially leading to unauthorized access and data breaches. Affected systems include web servers with outdated or vulnerable software, such as Apache or Nginx, and versions with known vulnerabilities. To remediate, defenders should update their web servers to the latest versions, such as Apache 2.4.49 or Nginx 1.21.6, and configure web application firewalls to detect and block suspicious traffic. Additionally, defenders should monitor system logs for unusual command execution and restrict access to sensitive directories.

items.title: Suspected cyberattack triggers false emergency alerts across parts of Brazil
items.description: The incident occurred early Saturday when at least a dozen unauthorized alerts were sent through Brazil's Civil Defense Alert system, a platform designed to warn residents about imminent threats such as floods, landslides and other natural disasters.

items.title: A Glimpse into the “Search Your Target” Market for Stolen Credentials
items.description: Attackers no longer need to sift through massive credential dumps. They can pay others to do it for them. Flare explores how an emerging underground market searches stolen credential databases for specific companies, domains, and accounts. [...]

items.title: Decades-Old Squid Proxy Flaw ‘Squidbleed’ Can Expose User Data
items.description: Squidbleed, discovered with the aid of Claude Mythos Preview, has been described as a Heartbleed-style vulnerability. The post Decades-Old Squid Proxy Flaw ‘Squidbleed’ Can Expose User Data appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: New OXLOADER Loader Uses Malicious Google Ads to Deliver CastleStealer
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have disclosed details of a new campaign that delivers CastleStealer by means of a previously unreported malware loader dubbed OXLOADER. According to Elastic Security Labs, the campaign leverages malicious Google Ads as a starting point to distribute the malware. Evidence indicates that the threat actor is likely Russian-speaking and financially motivated, owing to the

items.title: Google Sets Sept. 30 Deadline for Android Developer Verification in Four Countries
items.description: Google has set September 30, 2026, as the day it begins enforcing Android developer verification in the first four countries, and the major device-maker app stores are in from the start. On that date, certified Android phones in Brazil, Indonesia, Singapore, and Thailand will block normal installs of apps whose developers have not registered an identity with Google, whether the app

items.title: Stop Your Legacy Infrastructure from Hijacking Your AI Agents
items.description: Earlier this month, I spoke at the Gartner Security & Risk Management Summit about a blind spot most security programs are still not accounting for - how attackers are circumventing AI security programs by using legacy infrastructure to hijack AI agents. AI adoption is moving faster than security programs can account for. Roughly 71% of organizations are piloting AI agents across their

items.title: Attackers Exploit Gravity SMTP Plugin Flaw to Harvest Valuable WordPress Data
items.description: Vulnerable WordPress plugin iterations leak API keys, secrets, tokens, server information, and other data. The post Attackers Exploit Gravity SMTP Plugin Flaw to Harvest Valuable WordPress Data appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: North Korean Hackers Blamed for Mastra NPM Supply Chain Attack
items.description: A malicious dependency the attackers added to over 140 Mastra packages fetches a payload targeting cryptocurrency extensions. The post North Korean Hackers Blamed for Mastra NPM Supply Chain Attack appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: ⚡ Weekly Recap: Browser Bugs, EDR Killers, TV Botnet, OpenBSD Flaw, Android Trojan, and More
items.description: It’s Monday again. This week’s threat list looks painfully familiar: abused integrations, fake tools, poisoned websites, ransomware crews trying to shut down security tools, and mobile malware asking for way too much control. The annoying part is how little of this feels new. Weak credentials, sketchy downloads, browser extensions with too much access, and WordPress sites are used to push more

items.title: What the Latest ShinyHunters Breaches Reveal About Modern Cyberattacks
items.description: Groups like ShinyHunters are demonstrating that attackers do not necessarily need malware or zero-day exploits to cause massive damage. The post What the Latest ShinyHunters Breaches Reveal About Modern Cyberattacks appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: New Exploit Bypasses Apple’s Boot Defenses, Affects Millions of iPhones
items.description: The vulnerability exploited by the Usbliter8 exploit cannot be patched and a PoC exploit has been released by researchers. The post New Exploit Bypasses Apple’s Boot Defenses, Affects Millions of iPhones appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.description: A database of over 86,000 confirmed working credentials was created during the credential-harvesting campaign. The post Fortinet Responds to FortiBleed Campaign appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Canada’s Spy Agency Used First-of-Its-Kind Warrant to Clean Botnet-Infected Devices
items.description: Canada's spy service got a judge's permission to reach into infected servers, home routers, and IoT gear sitting on Canadian soil and neutralize two foreign-run botnets. The Federal Court released a public version of the ruling on June 15. It is the first time the Canadian Security Intelligence Service has used its threat reduction warrant powers this way. The warrant let CSIS alter,

items.title: More Cybersecurity Firms Disclose Impact From Klue Hack
items.description: HackerOne, Huntress, Jamf, OneTrust, Recorded Future, Snyk, and Tanium are among the affected Klue customers. The post More Cybersecurity Firms Disclose Impact From Klue Hack appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: AryStinger Malware Infects 4,300 Legacy Routers to Build Reconnaissance Proxy Network
items.description: A new malware family is turning forgotten home routers into a distributed reconnaissance and proxy network, not the DDoS botnet these devices usually end up in. QiAnXin's XLab calls it AryStinger and counts at least 4,300 infected routers, a total it says is still rising. The distinction matters. AryStinger exists for the stage of an attack that comes before the break-in. Infected

items.description: Writing on LinkedIn, Edwards said that while he has not agreed with how the investigation into him has been conducted, he has come to accept that his position “has become untenable.”

items.title: eBanking Phishing Delivered Through IPv4-Mapped IPv6 Address, (Fri, Jun 19th)
items.description: I detected an interesting phishing email this morning. It targets a major Belgian bank:

items.title: Novo Nordisk Breach Highlights Software Development Pipeline Risk
items.description: A leaked GitHub token underscores what most organizations get wrong: Treating secrets management as a tooling problem rather than an identity problem.

items.title: Bulgaria allowed surveillance tech firm to sell products to repressive regimes, report says
items.description: The nonprofit Human Rights Watch obtained export licensing records covering 2018 through 2023, which show the Bulgarian government allowed the surveillance firm Circles to peddle the tech to law enforcement and intelligence agencies in several countries known for human rights abuses.

items.title: Operation Escaneo Signals Shift in LatAm Threat Landscape
items.description: The threat group's curious business model may combine opportunistic monetization alongside intel collection, without much coordination between the two.

items.title: FIFA Bug Exposes World Cup Streams to Remote Takeover
items.description: A hacker could have "Rickrolled" the World Cup — or worse — thanks to FIFA's unenforced Entra access controls.

items.title: ‘Popa’ Botnet Linked to Publicly-Traded Israeli Firm
items.description: For the past four years, a sprawling Android-based botnet called Popa has forced millions of consumer TV boxes to relay Internet traffic linked to advertising fraud, account takeovers, and mass data-scraping efforts. This week, researchers from multiple security firms concluded that the Popa botnet is linked to NetNut, a "residential proxy" provider operated by the publicly-traded Israeli firm Alarum Technologies Ltd [NASDAQ: ALAR].

items.title: Salesforce Data Thefts Continue via Klue App Compromise
items.description: Klue's Battlecards is now the third integrated application that has been compromised to steal customers' Salesforce data, and victims include Huntress, the cybersecurity vendor.

items.title: Australian sugar producer works to restore operations as ransomware group claims attack
items.description: Mackay Sugar said it was "working urgently" to verify claims that a highly active ransomware group was behind a cyberattack that shut down harvesting and milling operations.

items.title: Get Out of Security Debt by Tackling the Exposure Problem
items.description: Teams digging out of security debt need to answer only two simple questions: Which vulnerabilities in our systems are exposed, and how long should they stay that way?

items.title: EU Gets a Head Start in Developing 6G Network Security
items.description: "Shield-6G" will combine AI threat detection, digital twins, honeypots, and more, to help carriers protect 6G networks against the threats of tomorrow.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Thursday, June 18th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9978, (Thu, Jun 18th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: The Behavior of Coordinated SSH Brute Force Attacks over the last three months [Guest Diary], (Wed, Jun 17th)
items.description: [This is a Guest Diary by Adam Nason, an ISC intern as part of the SANS.edu BACS program]

items.title: INC Ransomware Thrives by Mastering the Basics
items.description: And one of those basics is focusing on sectors where a ransomware disruption creates immediate pressure to pay up, like with healthcare.

items.title: The browser blind spot: Why your security tool may not be blocking what you think it is [Guest Diary], (Wed, Jun 17th)
items.description: [This is a guest diary submitted by Varun Murdula]

items.title: Sweeping Credential-Harvesting Heist Compromises 30K+ Fortinet Devices
items.description: Attackers are actively targeting various sectors across nearly 200 countries and already have compiled a list of working credentials for tens of thousands of compromised devices.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Wednesday, June 17th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9976, (Wed, Jun 17th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: From a VHDX File to a Remcos RAT, (Tue, Jun 16th)
items.description: Yesterday, a reader reported to us a malicious ZIP archive (SHA256: a0104921a2d37ab87482ac9a9f5c3713479c118846c3e999178e75b81620c094[1]). Once unzipped, it contains a VHDX file that discloses a malicious JavaScript after being mounted (which is automatic on modern Windows OSs):

items.title: Who Runs the Ransomware Group ‘The Gentlemen?’
items.description: A cybercrime group known as The Gentlemen has emerged as the second most active ransomware gang by victim count, rapidly attracting a talented pool of hackers through an aggressive recruitment strategy that promises affiliates 90 percent of any ransom paid by victims. This post examines clues pointing to a real life identity for the administrator of The Gentlemen ransomware group.

items.title: A Record-Breaking Patch Tuesday for June 2026
items.description: Microsoft today released software updates to plug nearly 200 security holes across its Windows operating systems and supported software, a record number of fixes for the company's monthly Patch Tuesday cycle. Nearly three dozen of those bugs earned Microsoft's most dire "critical" rating, and exploit code for at least three of the weaknesses is now publicly available.

items.title: Hackers Used Meta’s AI Support Bot to Seize Instagram Accounts
items.description: The Instagram accounts for the Obama White House and the Chief Master Sergeant of the U.S. Space Force were briefly defaced with pro-Iranian images and messages over the weekend, after instructions began circulating on Telegram showing how to trick Meta's "AI support assistant" bot into resetting account passwords.

items.title: Netherlands Seizes 800 Servers, Arrests 2 for Aiding Cyberattacks
items.description: Authorities in the Netherlands have arrested the co-owners of two related Internet hosting companies for operating IT infrastructure used by Russia to carry out cyberattacks, influence operations and disinformation campaigns inside the European Union. The two men were the focus of a 2025 KrebsOnSecurity story about how their hosting companies had assumed control over the technical infrastructure of Stark Industries Solutions, an Internet service provider sanctioned last year by the EU as a frequent staging ground for cyber mischief from Russia's intelligence agencies.

items.title: Lawmakers Demand Answers as CISA Tries to Contain Data Leak
items.description: Lawmakers in both houses of Congress are demanding answers from the U.S. Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) after KrebsOnSecurity reported this week that a CISA contractor intentionally published AWS GovCloud keys and a vast trove of other agency secrets on a public GitHub account. The inquiry comes as CISA is still struggling to contain the breach and invalidate the leaked credentials.

items.title: Alleged Kimwolf Botmaster ‘Dort’ Arrested, Charged in U.S. and Canada
items.description: Canadian authorities on Wednesday arrested a 23-year-old Ottawa man on suspicion of building and operating Kimwolf, a fast spreading Internet-of-Things botnet that enslaved millions of devices for use in a series of massive distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks over the past six months. KrebsOnSecurity publicly named the suspect in February 2026 after the accused launched a volley of DDoS, doxing and swatting campaigns against this author and a security researcher. He now faces criminal hacking charges in both Canada and the United States.

items.title: CISA Admin Leaked AWS GovCloud Keys on Github
items.description: Until this past weekend, a contractor for the Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) maintained a public GitHub repository that exposed credentials to several highly privileged AWS GovCloud accounts and a large number of internal CISA systems. Security experts said the public archive included files detailing how CISA builds, tests and deploys software internally, and that it represents one of the most egregious government data leaks in recent history.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, May 2026 Edition
items.description: Artificial intelligence platforms may be just as susceptible to social engineering as human beings, but they are proving remarkably good at finding security vulnerabilities in human-made computer code. That reality is on full display this month with some of the more widely-used software makers -- including Apple, Google, Microsoft, Mozilla and Oracle -- fixing near record volumes of security bugs, and/or quickening the tempo of their patch releases.

items.title: Canvas Breach Disrupts Schools & Colleges Nationwide
items.description: An ongoing data extortion attack targeting the widely-used education technology platform Canvas disrupted classes and coursework at school districts and universities across the United States today, after a cybercrime group defaced the service's login page with a ransom demand that threatened to leak data from 275 million students and faculty across nearly 9,000 educational institutions.

items.title: Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
